----
'''Level 999.99: “Haqq's Truth”''' ----


----
[[File:Level999.99-1.jpg|500px|link=]]

“LEST WE BE DISPERSED”

I used to think dispersion meant distance. Now I know it means subtraction. * * * I don't know how long I walked. Just that the pull in my ribs kept me moving. [[Entity 9|Facelings]] drifted in and out of my sight. Some of them spoke in languages I didn’t understand. Others stayed silent. When the [[Level 999|tower]] was finally within distance, it didn’t feel like I found it. It felt like it found ''me''. At the base was a [[Level 1000|door]]. It was huge, even from afar. It opened slowly. The hinges were quiet, but I could ''feel'' them—like the way you can feel a storm gathering before the first drop falls. My body braced for something to come through. Nothing did. The door was wide enough to enter, but I was too nervous. And I did that stupid thing— That human thing— That thing that gets people killed in this [[Backrooms Wiki|world]]— I hesitated. I was tired of paying for hope with my skin. So I took a step back. Just ''one'' step. And then— In the blink of an eye— The door vanished. * * * My eyes never left the space. I stood in the same desert, but everything else was different. The sky was clear. The [[Entity 9|facelings]] were gone. And the tower? It was different too. It was far, far away. I tried to run towards it. It never came any closer. I kept running. And running. And running. And it was still in the same spot. It wasn’t close enough to loom, nor was it far enough to be a part of the horizon. It just sat at an impossible distance. The path I walked earlier had vanished. The path I walked just now vanished too. No footprints. No trails. No nothing. Just flat, white sand in every direction. I circled. Over, and over again. I couldn’t stop. Tears stung my eyes, and they flowed down my cheeks. I collapsed onto my knees, and curled up on the ground. Salty droplets darkened the sand, and I sobbed for several minutes. Humiliating—even with no one to witness it. I eventually calmed down. My back faced the ground, and my gaze caught the sky. That was when I heard something. It was… thin, dry, intimate. Like someone had taken a thread and pulled it across the world. It rang once. Twice. Then thrice. “H— Hello?” My voice was raspy, cracking. … … … “Do not throw your breath at the sand.” Something spoke. It wasn’t loud, and it didn’t need to be. It hit my chest, like it was waiting for me to calm down. I froze. My eyes searched the sky. Searched the desert. Searched the tower. Nothing. I saw nothing but distance. “I— I can’t see you!” … … … And the voice returned. “That is mercy.” “What?” The voice took its time, like it had to cross a country to reach me. “If you could see me, then you would build something. You would kneel to what you can touch. You would become a slave to the [[Level 999|tower]].” I swallowed. My throat felt raw—sore. Like I’d been shouting for the past hour. “What is this place?” My words wobbled. The voice answered after a long, patient pause. “This is the distance between your worth and what you have been taught to trade for it.” That sentence struck so cleanly I felt dizzy. I stared up at the tower. It looked like a stack of dried blood against the sky. It wasn't the same as I saw earlier. “Are you… up there?” I asked. … … “No.” “Then where are you?” … … “Far enough that you can’t use me as an excuse. Close enough that you can’t use loneliness as one.” That response made me laugh—sharp, disbelieving. It felt sour in my mouth. I hated it. It felt disgusting. “What do you know about me?” … … “I know what you call yourself.” My grin faded. I wasn’t sure what the voice was talking about. “You call yourself… ‘disposable’ when you are not wanted.” … … … My throat tightened. “That's not—” I started, and the words caught. I didn’t even know what I was denying. The voice didn’t press. It just waited, like it had all the time in the world. Then— “Pick up a handful of sand, and stand up.” I didn’t. Not right away. I stared at my palms like it was a trick—like the desert was about to laugh at me for trying. Then, I finally crouched, scooping up a fistful. The sand was cold, sifting between my fingers—too fine, too eager to escape. “Hold it,” the voice instructed. I clenched my fist. The sand bit into my palm. “Now, open your hand.” I loosened my fist. The sand poured out with a soft hiss. “So?” I muttered. … … “That sand is a brick.” “…What?” … … “A brick,” the voice repeated. “You can stack it.
You can shape it.
You can call it important because it becomes a component of the tower.
And when it is not stacked—when it is not shaped—when it is not part of the tower—” The voice paused longer than usual. It felt deliberate. “—you think it becomes nothing.” I stared at the sand, still clinging to my skin. “What are you trying to get at?” I asked. “Are you trying to say I’m like sand?” Pause. “Of course not. You are the one who notices it is cold.” That sentence made my eyes sting. I hated it for landing gently. I hated it because it made sense. I hated it because it was true. I wiped my face with my sleeves. “What am I supposed to do then? Walk back? Find the door? Finish the tower’s little schtick?” The voice didn't answer immediately. Instead, that sound from before came. Again. And again. Seven times, spaced apart. When the seventh note faded, the voice returned. “Sit.” I didn’t want to, but I still did. I sank into the pale sand. The voice—still unseen, unreachable—spoke. “Tell me what you think you are worth.” My mouth went dry. I could have lied. I knew how to lie. I survived on lying. But I didn’t this time. I didn’t lie. “…I don’t know.” The words didn’t crack. They stayed whole, ugly as they were. Pause. “You do know,” the voice replied. “You just cannot see it yet.” Heat crawled up my neck. “I’m worth… what I can do,” I said, bitter. “What I can carry. What I can fix. How fast I can run. How long I can stay awake. How quiet I can be. How little I can need.” My words trembled. They tasted like metal— No. Like blood. The voice waited, then asked gently, “And if you cannot do those things?” My stomach twisted. I tried to answer, but I couldn’t find the words I wanted. So I said the easiest thing I could say. “I’d be dead.” That one stayed whole. The tower in the distance seemed to sharpen, as if listening. Then, the voice spoke, softer—so soft it felt like compassion. “That is what this [[Backrooms Wiki|world]] teaches.” I stared at my hands. The sand had left tiny cuts in my palm. The voice continued, “It teaches you to build a name so you can pretend you are not afraid of being forgotten.” I flinched. I thought of the [[Entity 9|facelings]] I passed by. They were frolicking, mumbling gibberish to one another. I thought of all the people who failed to find safety in this [[Backrooms Wiki|world]], their bodies left as checkpoints for the next unlucky few. The tower— No. This [[Backrooms Wiki|world]] wanted bodies. It wanted effort. It wanted devotion. It didn't care what it cost you. I spoke softly, “Then what do you teach?” The voice took its time again. “I teach you to see value—value in yourself.” “You say that, but... you’re not even [[:Category:Cluster I|here]], are you?” I asked. The voice didn't sound offended. “I am as far as I need to be. If I were near, you would bargain with me. You would trade suffering for praise. You would try to earn worth as if it were a wage.” My eyes burned. “That’s... that's all I've ever done.” ... ... “Then let us practice not disappearing," the voice said. My hands clenched. “How?” My breath grew heavy, anticipating what the voice would say next. “Say one sentence that is true—even if no one rewards you for it.” That request made my chest seize. My mind searched for something safe. Something that I wouldn't feel humiliated for sharing. Something that would keep me from feeling the full weight of myself. Something clever. But every sentence I reached for crumbled before it left my mouth. So I chose the one that hurt. “I don’t want to die.” That sentence held, and the sand felt a little warmer. It was still cold, but felt warmer in some way. “Again,” the voice instructed. I squeezed my eyes shut. “I’m tired,” I said. That warmth grew. “I’m scared,” I said. Grew. “I’m angry,” I said. And grew. “I miss home,” and my voice broke—not the words. Me. Just me. The voice waited through my breathing. Then— “Now, say a sentence you refuse to bargain with.” My hands trembled. My mouth felt full of ash. I opened my eyes and stared at the tower's impossible distance. I imagined the door opening without me. I imagined the light spilling out for someone else. I imagined myself stuck in this desert until I forgot how to speak. And in that fear, something stubborn rose up. Not courage. Not hope. Something quieter. “I matter.” Those words were small. They were not impressive. They did not build anything. But they stayed whole. And that was enough. When I spoke those words, the wind shifted. It was cold, chilling, but also comforting in an odd way. I turned my head towards the wind, and I saw something I hadn’t seen before. The tower wasn't the only structure out there. Far to my left—so far it might’ve been another lie—a circle of lights waited. Seven of them. Steady. Waiting. A gathering without the tower. My breath caught. “I see something.” Pause. “Good,” the voice responded. “I didn’t make it appear,” I asked quickly, afraid of being tricked. “Did I?” Pause. “Of course not,” the voice replied. “You only noticed it.” I rose to my feet slowly. No compulsion behind my ribs. No invisible hand shoving me forward. Only my own legs. My own body. My own choice. I took a step towards the circle of lights and looked back. Behind me, the tower remained at its impossible distance. Still present, still hungry. But it didn’t feel like my judge anymore. It felt like a lesson I could walk away from. I stopped and looked at the sky. “I don't know your name,” I said to the distance. A long pause—long enough that I thought the voice might’ve been gone. Then, it arrived. “You do not need it.” I swallowed. “What do I call you then?” The wind weakened. “Call me what you call the part you stopped listening to.” My throat tightened. I nodded, even though I had no idea if the voice could see me at all. “Will you come closer?” … … … “No. Not yet.” “Why?” The voice still took its time to respond, but it was a little quicker than usual. “Because you are still learning the difference between being saved and being seen.” I stood there a moment longer, letting the sentence settle in my mind. Then I turned towards the seven lights. I walked. Not because the voice demanded it. Not because the [[Level 999|level]] demanded it. Not because the [[Backrooms Wiki|world]] itself demanded it. But because I did.
[[File:The Glitched Void.jpg|500px|link=|alt=The Human Limit]]
